title: Cannot ssh to container for operation permitted on /proc/self/loginuid
body:
/kind bug

Regression

**Description**

I have a container with SSH running inside. I have added AUDIT_WRITE capability to the container. After updating podman to mainline, unable to SSH to the container.

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

1. Add "AUDIT_WRITE" to the default_capabilities list in /usr/share/containers/containers.conf file

2. Put SELinux in Permissive mode

3. Start a container with public IP address and run sshd inside

4. Login to the container using the public IP address

**Describe the results you received:**
SSH connection breaks as soon after credentials is verified and PAM tries to open session.

/var/log/secure log
```
Jan 25 20:12:17 myhost sshd[278]: Accepted password for myuser from 1x.yy.aa.bb port 51250 ssh2
Jan 25 20:12:17 myhost sshd[278]: pam_loginuid(sshd:session): Error writing /proc/self/loginuid: Operation not permitted
Jan 25 20:12:17 myhost sshd[278]: pam_loginuid(sshd:session): set_loginuid failed
Jan 25 20:12:17 myhost sshd[278]: pam_unix(sshd:session): session opened for user myuser by (uid=0)
Jan 25 20:12:17 myhost sshd[278]: error: PAM: pam_open_session(): Cannot make/remove an entry for the specified session
Jan 25 20:12:17 myhost sshd[280]: Received disconnect 1x.yy.aa.bb port 51250:11: disconnected by user
Jan 25 20:12:17 myhost sshd[280]: Disconnected from 1x.yy.aa.bb port 51250
```

```

debug1: session_by_channel: session 0 channel 0

Starting session: shell on pts/2 for myuser from 1x.yy.aa.bb port 51228 id 0
```

**Describe the results you expected:**
SSH connection should work.

**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**
Always

comments.body:
you need to have `CAP_AUDIT_WRITE` in the initial user namespace.  How was the container created?

Is `loginuid_immutable` set?  You can verify it with `auditctl -s`
and for the container use case, you also need `CAP_AUDIT_CONTROL`, since it is required to change the `loginuid` once it is set

an example:

```
# podman run --cap-add AUDIT_WRITE --rm fedora sh -c 'echo 2 > /proc/1/loginuid && echo ok'
sh: line 1: echo: write error: Operation not permitted
# podman run --cap-add AUDIT_CONTROL --rm fedora sh -c 'echo 2 > /proc/1/loginuid && echo ok'
ok
```
given the above, I am closing the issue, but feel free to comment further and reopen if there is still something we should look at

@giuseppe ,
Thank you for the pointer. With AUDIT_CONTROL capability, the SSH session does work.

The SSH used to work without any additional capability podman 3.0.x. With 3.3.1, it needed AUDIT_WRITE capability. With the mainline (4.0), it requires AUDIT_CONTROL. What are the security implications of granting this capability to containers?

What changes have happened in podman for which these capability requirements are changing with podman versions?

I don't think it depends from podman, was the kernel changed as well?

Adding capabilities reduce the security, it would be better if you didn't need to, but in your case I am not sure how to achieve that, is the error fatal or ssh still work?  If it is just noise, I would ignore it and not add capabilities.  Another possibility is to teach sshd it is running in a container and ignore such failures

In podman 3.0 we gave every container AUDIT_WRITE by default, we don't do that in podman 3.4, since the only containers needing it are SSH containers, which is kind of an anti-pattern.
AUDIT_CONTROL allows the processes within the container to modify the Audit subsystem, for people who care about audit this is a very big deal, for those that don't it is not.

but that won't still explain why AUDIT_WRITE was enough before.

I've looked at the kernel and it seems AUDIT_CONTROL was always required to change the loginuid.

How was it working before without that capability?

maybe it depended on something else, since I can reproduce the same error on CentOS Stream 8:

```
# podman --version
podman version 3.0.2-dev

# podman run --rm fedora sh -c 'getpcaps 1'
1: cap_chown,cap_dac_override,cap_fowner,cap_fsetid,cap_kill,cap_setgid,cap_setuid,cap_setpcap,cap_net_bind_service,cap_net_raw,cap_sys_chroot,cap_setfcap=eip

#  podman run --rm fedora sh -c 'echo 2 > /proc/self/loginuid'
sh: line 1: echo: write error: Operation not permitted
```

OpenSSH versions, maybe?

I encountered this on a personal system - I had an image based on an old (~29-30) Fedora release, that I finally upgraded to F35. When I recreated the container, the old command-line invocation didn't work after the upgrade, and I had to add the `AUDIT_CONTROL` capability to restore functionality.

I can tell that the SSH version (openssh-7.4p1-21.el7.x86_64) inside the container stays same while updating the podman version. The host SSH version, that should not matter in this case, is also same when updating podman version from 3.3.1 to mainline.
/reopen

@vikas-goel: Reopened this issue.

<details>

In response to [this](https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/13012#issuecomment-1022591352):

>/reopen


Instructions for interacting with me using PR comments are available [here](https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/guide/pull-requests.md).  If you have questions or suggestions related to my behavior, please file an issue against the [kubernetes/test-infra](https://github.com/kubernetes/test-infra/issues/new?title=Prow%20issue:) repository.
</details>

I told you we tightened the security with the express understanding that this would break sshd inside of the container.

You have to add the CAP_AUDIT_WRITE.

The CAP_AUDIT_CONTROL, should not cause issues.
If you want to change back to the previous defaults you can go modify the default cap list in containers.conf.

I added the AUDIT_WRITE capability in the conf file that worked with podman 3.3.1 (default version of RHEL8.5).

However, after updating just the podman to mainline (no other change in the system), it requires additional AUDIT_CONTROL capability without which SSH session does not open.

That makes no sense, since neither version ever allowed AUDIT_CONTROL. Something else has changed.  Unless we are leaking information into the container which makes the container think it can set AUDIT_CONTROL.
Are you running this container by hand or under systemd?
Manually always using podman run command

I've tried installing openssh-server from Fedora 35 in a container and used systemd to start it.  I've traced it and I don't see it is attempting to write to `/proc/self/loginuid` if it has already a value:

```
[pid 1047883] openat(AT_FDCWD, "/proc/self/loginuid", O_RDWR|O_NOFOLLOW) = 3
[pid 1047883] read(3, "1000", 24)       = 4
[pid 1047883] read(3, "", 20)           = 0
[pid 1047883] close(3)                  = 0
```

This is the Containerfile I've used:

```
FROM fedora
RUN yum install -y systemd openssh-server && yum clean all -y \
&& systemctl enable sshd \
&& adduser foo -p '$y$j9T$qs3an4DwYukN5egjZMvQe0$lrrYNXm/zCXqMqwAK9bZ551tYUAXth9pHOyTLjdaXJ/'
```

The password is `foo`

```
# podman run -d  --cap-add AUDIT_WRITE -p 2222:22  --rm fedora-ssh /usr/lib/systemd/systemd
70823234a41463b426600edd43c7a21f14ff6e35e683439abc459994929ea4e2

$ ssh -o UserKnownHostsFile=/dev/null -o StrictHostKeyChecking=no -p 2222 foo@localhost
Warning: Permanently added '[localhost]:2222' (ED25519) to the list of known hosts.
foo@localhost's password: 
[foo@70823234a414 ~]$ 
```

It even works with rootless.

I believe loginuid would only be set on a new login user, iff the loginuid is set to 0 before the user is launched.
If a user launches sshd (even via a container) then the sshd would have the users loginuid, and when it launches a process, would not attempt to write the loginuid.  (AT least that is my understanding on how this is supposed to work).
If loginuid is set to 0 (being launched directly from systemd), then I would think sshd would attempt to set the loginuid.
/reopen

<details>

In response to [this](https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/13012#issuecomment-1033288163):

>/reopen

If you have questions or suggestions related to my behavior, please file an issue against the [kubernetes/test-infra](https://github.com/kubernetes/test-infra/issues/new?title=Prow%20issue:) repository.
</details>

I tried this with the latest podman mainline on vanilla RHEL8.5. But still running into the issue same issue without AUDIT_CONTROL capability.

Feb  8 18:45:39 flex sshd[276]: Accepted password for admin from 10.x.y.z port 52920 ssh2
Feb  8 18:45:39 flex sshd[276]: pam_loginuid(sshd:session): Error writing /proc/self/loginuid: Operation not permitted
Feb  8 18:45:39 flex sshd[276]: pam_loginuid(sshd:session): set_loginuid failed
Feb  8 18:45:39 flex sshd[276]: pam_unix(sshd:session): session opened for user admin by (uid=0)
Feb  8 18:45:39 flex sshd[276]: error: PAM: pam_open_session(): Cannot make/remove an entry for the specified session
Feb  8 18:45:39 flex sshd[278]: Received disconnect from 10.x.y.z port 52920:11: disconnected by user
Feb  8 18:45:39 flex sshd[278]: Disconnected from 10.x.y.z port 52920


Client:       Podman Engine
Version:      4.0.0-dev
API Version:  4.0.0-dev
Go Version:   go1.17.5
Git Commit:   6ffd59828dd3c58c0927bd715e0a5a7b0c3baf2f
Built:        Tue Feb  8 11:17:08 2022
OS/Arch:      linux/amd64

As I mentioned earlier, it was working with podman 3.3.1. It works with Docker too with exactly same container image without adding AUDIT_CONTROL capability.

From Docker container where SSH works:
```
$ capsh --print
Current: = cap_chown,cap_dac_override,cap_fowner,cap_fsetid,cap_kill,cap_setgid,cap_setuid,cap_setpcap,cap_net_bind_service,cap_net_raw,cap_sys_chroot,cap_sys_admin,cap_mknod,cap_audit_write,cap_setfcap+i
Bounding set =cap_chown,cap_dac_override,cap_fowner,cap_fsetid,cap_kill,cap_setgid,cap_setuid,cap_setpcap,cap_net_bind_service,cap_net_raw,cap_sys_chroot,cap_sys_admin,cap_mknod,cap_audit_write,cap_setfcap
Securebits: 00/0x0/1'b0
 secure-noroot: no (unlocked)
 secure-no-suid-fixup: no (unlocked)
 secure-keep-caps: no (unlocked)
uid=1000(appadmin)
gid=100(users)
groups=0(root),10(wheel),100(users)
```

From Podman (mainline) container where SSH does not work:
```
bash-4.2# capsh --print
Current: = cap_chown,cap_dac_override,cap_fowner,cap_fsetid,cap_kill,cap_setgid,cap_setuid,cap_setpcap,cap_net_bind_service,cap_net_raw,cap_sys_chroot,cap_audit_write,cap_setfcap+eip
Bounding set =cap_chown,cap_dac_override,cap_fowner,cap_fsetid,cap_kill,cap_setgid,cap_setuid,cap_setpcap,cap_net_bind_service,cap_net_raw,cap_sys_chroot,cap_audit_write,cap_setfcap
Securebits: 00/0x0/1'b0
 secure-noroot: no (unlocked)
 secure-no-suid-fixup: no (unlocked)
 secure-keep-caps: no (unlocked)
uid=0(root)
gid=0(root)
groups=
bash-4.2#
```

In both cases the command output is from inside the container with exactly same image.
the docker container has CAP_SYS_ADMIN, I believe Podman will work in the same way if you add CAP_SYS_ADMIN (that is almost equivalent to running as root on the host).

Podman container with SYS_ADMIN capability didn't help. Received exactly same error with the following container.
```
# podman exec -it mycontainer capsh --print
Current: = cap_chown,cap_dac_override,cap_fowner,cap_fsetid,cap_kill,cap_setgid,cap_setuid,cap_setpcap,cap_net_bind_service,cap_net_raw,cap_sys_chroot,cap_sys_admin,cap_audit_write,cap_setfcap+eip
Bounding set =cap_chown,cap_dac_override,cap_fowner,cap_fsetid,cap_kill,cap_setgid,cap_setuid,cap_setpcap,cap_net_bind_service,cap_net_raw,cap_sys_chroot,cap_sys_admin,cap_audit_write,cap_setfcap
Securebits: 00/0x0/1'b0
 secure-noroot: no (unlocked)
 secure-no-suid-fixup: no (unlocked)
 secure-keep-caps: no (unlocked)
uid=0(root)
gid=0(root)
groups=
```

On the other hand, I can SSH in Docker environment without SYS_ADMIN capability
```
$ id
uid=1000(appadmin) gid=100(users) groups=100(users),0(root),10(wheel)
$ capsh --print
Current: = cap_chown,cap_dac_override,cap_fowner,cap_fsetid,cap_kill,cap_setgid,cap_setuid,cap_setpcap,cap_net_bind_service,cap_net_raw,cap_sys_chroot,cap_mknod,cap_audit_write,cap_setfcap+i
Bounding set =cap_chown,cap_dac_override,cap_fowner,cap_fsetid,cap_kill,cap_setgid,cap_setuid,cap_setpcap,cap_net_bind_service,cap_net_raw,cap_sys_chroot,cap_mknod,cap_audit_write,cap_setfcap
Securebits: 00/0x0/1'b0
 secure-noroot: no (unlocked)
 secure-no-suid-fixup: no (unlocked)
 secure-keep-caps: no (unlocked)
uid=1000(appadmin)
gid=100(users)
groups=0(root),10(wheel),100(users)
```
/reopen

@vikas-goel: Reopened this issue.

<details>

In response to [this](https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/13012#issuecomment-1033986608):

>/reopen


Instructions for interacting with me using PR comments are available [here](https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/guide/pull-requests.md).  If you have questions or suggestions related to my behavior, please file an issue against the [kubernetes/test-infra](https://github.com/kubernetes/test-infra/issues/new?title=Prow%20issue:) repository.
</details>

First, largest observation: UID and GID are different in the Podman container and Docker container. Were the containers in Docker vs Podman started differently?

The capabilities are definitely different as well, will get a more detailed diff

Differences are:
Docker has `cap_mknod` and `cap_setfcap+i` - Podman has `cap_sys_admin` and `cap_setfcap+eip`
what is the content of `/proc/$PID/loginuid` for the current shell where you launch podman and for the dockerd process?

Yup, the issue is if /proc/$PID/loginuid is set, IE not -1, then modifying this field requires AUDIT_CONTROL. 
If the loginuid you are attempting to change is -1, then you ownly need AUDIT_WRITE.

Since dockerd is started by systemd, it will work, and since Podman is started by a logged in user, it will not.

If you setup podman.socket and podman.service and run your container with podman --remote, it will probably work.

> First, largest observation: UID and GID are different in the Podman container and Docker container. Were the containers in Docker vs Podman started differently?
> 
> The capabilities are definitely different as well, will get a more detailed diff

The UID and GID are different because I had to run `capsh` in Podman using `podman exec` as I was not able to SSH. Running that command landed me to `root` account. Whereas, in case of Docker I ran the same `capsh` command after doing SSH to the container as non-root user.
If I run the `capsh` command using `docker exec`, then the UID/GID will be same.

> Differences are: Docker has `cap_mknod` and `cap_setfcap+i` - Podman has `cap_sys_admin` and `cap_setfcap+eip`

The cap_sys_admin is not making any difference. SSH is possible in Docker env with and without this capability. Whereas, cannot SSH in Podman in either case.

Will other capability differences make any change in ability to SSH?

> what is the content of `/proc/$PID/loginuid` for the current shell where you launch podman and for the dockerd process?

It's 0 for podman and 4294967295 for dockerd

> Yup, the issue is if /proc/$PID/loginuid is set, IE not -1, then modifying this field requires AUDIT_CONTROL. If the loginuid you are attempting to change is -1, then you ownly need AUDIT_WRITE.
> 
> Since dockerd is started by systemd, it will work, and since Podman is started by a logged in user, it will not.
> 
> If you setup podman.socket and podman.service and run your container with podman --remote, it will probably work.

This works. Is there a configuration parameter that can be tuned to achieve the same without running podman.socket and podman.service (and --remote)?
you may need to tweak the pam configuration to not require `pam_loginuid.so`

Host level configuration change is in the control, not inside the container. Is there is a way to achieve with host configuration change?
If not, can we take this is an RFE?
How shall I proceed? Create a new RFE or convert this one?

I don't think there is anything we should do in Podman to address it.

The configuration could be done on the host too, if ssh on the host doesn't write to loginuid then the container is free to do so (but I'd suggest to not do it).

Another alternative is to delegate the container startup to a systemd unit, like: `systemd-run podman run ....`

Opened a PR to document it: https://github.com/containers/podman/pull/13197
Does SSHD actually fail, or does it print an error?
We might want this to be a bug in sshd.
BTW I wish that the kernel would just block setting the loginuid altogether, I think it ruins the security of the system allowing any process to modify this.

I'm very sorry that I made a mistake yesterday. The method I provided does not solve this issue when I repeated the test today. I made a mistake that I forgot I added AUDIT_WRITE to the podman configuration so I thought it was the solution but it turns out that SyslogFacility is not the key point. Sorry for wasting your time :(

~~Additional notes:~~

~~Besides enabling `AUDIT_WRITE`, there is another way to solve this problem as follows.~~

~~Edit the `/etc/ssh/sshd_confilg` file and find the following line:~~

~~SyslogFacility AUTHPRIV~~

~~and change it to~~

~~SyslogFacility AUTH~~


~~Then, sshd will no longer requires `AUDIT_WRITE` and it is totally acceptable for containers.~~

~~So write this line in the dockerfile:~~

~~RUN sed -i -e 's/^SyslogFacility AUTHPRIV$/SyslogFacility AUTH/' /etc/ssh/sshd_config~~

~~or the more robust one:~~

~~RUN sed -i -e 's/^\s*SyslogFacility\s\+AUTHPRIV\s*$/SyslogFacility AUTH/' /etc/ssh/sshd_config~~
Very nice would like to get this into the troublshoot.md file.

> BTW I wish that the kernel would just block setting the loginuid altogether, I think it ruins the security of the system allowing any process to modify this.

I think you can set `AUDIT_FEATURE_LOGINUID_IMMUTABLE` to disallow changes:
https://github.com/torvalds/linux/blob/e359bce39d9085ab24eaa0bb0778bb5f6894144a/Documentation/ABI/stable/procfs-audit_loginuid#L12-L15

Is this available as a sysctl or just at kernel build time?

I think this should be default in Fedora BTW.

> Is this available as a sysctl or just at kernel build time?

```auditctl --loginuid-immutable```
